title: CO₂ emissions per capita vs. fossil fuel consumption per capita
subtitle: Fossil fuel consumption is measured as the average consumption of energy from coal, oil and gas per person. [Fossil fuel and industry emissions](#dod:fossilemissions) are included. [Land-use change emissions](#dod:land-use-change-emissions) are not included.
caption:
CO₂ emissions per capita vs. fossil fuel consumption per capita
Fossil fuel consumption is measured as the average consumption of energy from coal, oil and gas per person. [Fossil fuel and industry emissions](#dod:fossilemissions) are included. [Land-use change emissions](#dod:land-use-change-emissions) are not included.

This scatter plot maps the relationship between per capita energy from fossil fuels, measured in kilowatt-hours on the horizontal axis, and per capita CO₂ emissions, measured in tonnes on the vertical axis. Both axes use a logarithmic scale to accommodate the wide range of values across the global population. The data points are color-coded by income group: pink for lower-middle-income countries, light green for upper-middle-income countries, and dark green for high-income countries. Circle size corresponds to population, making the large circles for India and China visually distinct from smaller nations. A strong positive correlation is visible, where higher fossil fuel consumption coincides with higher emissions. At the lower end of the scale, Pakistan and the Philippines sit below 5,000 kWh and under 2 tonnes of emissions. Moving up the scale, India and Indonesia occupy the 10,000 kWh range with emissions between 2 and 3 tonnes. A dense cluster of high-income nations, including the United Kingdom, Germany, and Japan, groups around 20,000 to 30,000 kWh and 5 to 10 tonnes of emissions. The United States and South Korea appear further right, exceeding 50,000 kWh with emissions surpassing 10 tonnes. At the extreme upper right, Qatar, Kuwait, and Saudi Arabia show the highest values, with fossil fuel consumption exceeding 100,000 kWh and emissions reaching above 20 tonnes. Singapore also sits in this high-consumption, high-emission band. The visual arrangement shows that while population size varies significantly, the ratio of energy use to carbon output remains consistent across the different income groups represented.
